EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system situated to the north of the state proceeded east-southeast out of the Dakotas and into Minnesota through the day.  The accompanying cold front, while not excessively cold, did come with a 850mb jet of around 70kts.  Resulting sustained winds approached 40 mph in many locations across northern Iowa, including a number of mesonet and ASOS sites reporting wind gusts at or in excess of 58 mph in the mid to late morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: ASOS station in Estherville measured 61 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slowly eastward moving low pressure center resided in central South Dakota. By the afternoon hours of the 24th, Iowa found itself well within the warm sector with the warm front into southern Iowa and the cold and dry fronts back into central and eastern Nebraska.  Afternoon instability, while greatest to the west and southwest, did approach 1000 - 2000 J/kg.  The result was a few borderline severe storms and a couple of severe storms and associated reports across northern/northwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported wind gusts to around 65 mph.  Report came in via social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across the area spawning numerous severe thunderstorms with damaging winds during the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported several branches were down varying in size from 2 to 18.5  inches in diameter along Peartree Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across the area spawning numerous severe thunderstorms with damaging winds during the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The AWOS site at Washington reported a 54 Kt wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated thunderstorm formed in a moist, unstable air mass that produced one damaging wind report.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree a foot and a half in diameter was down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The back end of a passing cold front spawned an isolated severe thunderstorm in extreme southeastern Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported estimated winds of 50 to 60 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The back end of a passing cold front spawned an isolated severe thunderstorm in extreme southeastern Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported a large tree branch was down at the local residence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across portions of northeast Iowa during the evening of May 30th. These storms developed ahead of an approaching warm front and some of them produced severe wind gusts. Wind gusts to 58 and 59 mph were measured by the automated weather observing equipment at the Oelwein (Fayette County) airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 58 mph was measured by the automated weather observing equipment at the Oelwein airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front produced thunderstorms with some severe storms with damaging winds to over 70 MPH and some large hail up to the size of golf balls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds were reported to gust to about 60 MPH for roughly a minute.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed and strengthened over portions of central Nebraska and eastern Kansas through the day.  The warm front moved up into southern Minnesota while the weakening cold front existed over eastern Nebraska, leaving much of Iowa within the unstable warm sector.  Storms developed through the day along and near the warm sector, within the warm sector, and along the cold front.  Storms during the morning and early afternoon remained sub-severe, with storms mid-afternoon onward primarily producing damaging winds and heavy rainfall.  A brief tornado was reported in Bremer county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported trees and power lines damaged in town.  Reported by NWS employee.  Time estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wave riding the northwest upper level flow through the Dakotas and Northern Minnesota initiated a series of storms that eventually topped the ridge and started southeast.  The storms eventually initiated along an outflow boundary, taking them due south into northern Iowa, where they produced periods of heavy rain and a few damaging and high wind reports near Estherville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estherville airport ASOS reported a 51 kt wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wave riding the northwest upper level flow through the Dakotas and Northern Minnesota initiated a series of storms that eventually topped the ridge and started southeast.  The storms eventually initiated along an outflow boundary, taking them due south into northern Iowa, where they produced periods of heavy rain and a few damaging and high wind reports near Estherville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported via social media that trees were down, along with torrential rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The state was stuck under northwest flow aloft, resulting in shortwaves aloft initiating storms.  Additionally, at the surface a warm frontal boundary resided across the southwest third to half of the state during the day of the 21st and through the overnight.  Some storms initially developed during the afternoon and evening hours in the immediate vicinity of the front in southern and central Iowa and produced periods of heavy rainfall.  Storms primarily resulted in heavy rainfall and a couple of severe wind reports.  The primary action initiated late in the evening across the northwest third of the state, in the vicinity of the warm front as it moved northward.  Given northwest steering flow and a nearly station surface front, storms trained through the evening and resulted in rainfall totals as high as 3.71 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Algona airport AWOS measured wind gust of 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An ideal setup was seen across northern and central Iowa for repeated rounds of heavy rainfall and severe weather.  A weak warm frontal boundary stalled out across the area as it transitioned to a stationary boundary and allowed multiple rounds of storms to initiate on the 21st and well into the 22nd.  Primarily heavy rainfall and flooding was seen, however a couple of hail and a handful of damaging wind reports were received as well.  The hardest hit areas were from roughly Forest City on southeast towards Mason city and Waverly. Rainfall amounts of 4 to 6 plus inches were seen, with greatest amounts just outside of the Des Moines county warning area in and around Charles City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported 3 inch tree branches down along with quarter sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively weak cold front that worked through the state the previous day stalled out across northern Missouri and tracked back northward as a weak warm front and eventual stationary front.  Initially, storms were able to initiate in South Dakota along/north of the warm/stationary front and progress east/southeast through the evening and overnight hours of the 8th into the 9th.  Later that day, another round of storms moving out of Nebraska were able to strengthen with daytime heating and produce another round of heavy rainfall through portions of the state.  The greatest daily totals reached into the 4 to 5 inch range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large branch around 6 inches in diameter down on the Iowa State University campus west of the Food Science Building.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed over areas of western South Dakota and Nebraska during the afternoon of July 6th. These thunderstorms moved east and developed upscale into a large convective complex that moved into northeast Nebraska during the late evening on the 6th of July. The line of thunderstorms moved southeast across all of eastern Nebraska and western Iowa with the primary severe weather threat being from damaging winds. The thunderstorms exited into northeast Kansas and northern Missouri during the early morning. This complex of thunderstorms then left an outflow boundary across the area, which additional thunderstorms developed along during the afternoon of July 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The emergency manager reported that the roof from the local power plant was removed. A chicken plant on the southwest side of town was also destroyed, and there were numerous large tree branches down in town as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms develop late in the evening of July 17 in response to the nocturnal strengthening of the low-level jet over a stalled frontal boundary over southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa. Although the majority of these thunderstorms were strong, only isolated reports of severe weather were observed mainly in the form of strong winds. In addition to the severe weather, the orientation of the thunderstorms from northwest to southeast resulted in repeated or training of the storms and heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and numerous limbs were reported downed, some on power lines, was reported in the city of Clarinda.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of the 25th, a cold front progressed into portions of central and eastern Nebraska and eventually stalled out in eastern Nebraska.  During the evening hours storms that initiated earlier in the day in western Nebraska continued to make their way across Nebraska and into Iowa and produced a number of high wind, damaging wind, and hail reports.  Prior to the main line, a severe storm fired up in northern Iowa in Emmet and produced near severe hail and severe winds. After the main line pushed through in the overnight hours, a stalled front in northern Missouri aided in ushering in another round of heavy rainfall across southern Iowa, where reports upwards of 4 plus inches were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An Iowa DOT RWIS Mesonet station 1 mile east of Estherville reported a peak wind gust of 54 kts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary extended from northeast Nebraska through western and into southern Iowa. Nocturnal thunderstorms, which had developed over central Nebraska, moved into southwest Iowa by mid morning and as they approached the front they intensified producing severe weather in Shelby County before moving east of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The emergency manager reported that a tree fell on a car along Highway 59 just to the north of Harlan. There were no injuries reported in the vehicle.

Very strong winds developed over northwest Iowa on Christmas night and continued into the morning of December 26th. The southwest to west winds developed as a low pressure system, which had brought rain and thunderstorms on Christmas Day, moved just north of the area. Sustained winds reached up to 45 mph, and gusts were measured as high as 63 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds from a westerly direction followed a low pressure system which had brought Christmas Day rain and thunderstorms. There were several reports of power lines and branches down and resulting power outages, and traffic lights and some signs were damaged. The winds included estimated sustained winds of 40 mph and gusts to near 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cool front spawned widespread thunderstorms resulting in numerous reports of winds between 40 to 70 MPH along and north of I-80 in Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported 55 to 60 MPH winds and power out at his residence.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported several tree branches around 4 inches in diameter were down around the neighborhood.  The time was estimated based upon radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front produced thunderstorms with some severe storms with damaging winds to over 70 MPH and some large hail up to the size of golf balls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several tree branches and a flag pole were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system with an accompanying cool front produced showers and thunderstorms.  Several reports of damaging winds of 60 to 70 MPH occurred, along with isolated reports of large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The county emergency manager reported power lines down and the roof blown off a machine shed at the intersection of county road X44 and highway 64 south of Amber.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system with an accompanying cool front produced showers and thunderstorms.  Several reports of damaging winds of 60 to 70 MPH occurred, along with isolated reports of large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The fire department reported an estimated wind gust of 60 MPH and was relayed by the Dubuque County EOC.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system with an accompanying cool front produced showers and thunderstorms.  Several reports of damaging winds of 60 to 70 MPH occurred, along with isolated reports of large hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system with an accompanying cool front produced showers and thunderstorms.  Several reports of damaging winds of 60 to 70 MPH occurred, along with isolated reports of large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The emergency manager reported a two foot diameter tree was snapped off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disturbance interacting with a frontal system produced an area of severe thunderstorms with numerous damaging winds, some flash flooding and a few isolated, brief tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported via facebook several large trees and some branches were down.  The time was estimated using radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the early morning hours of July 7th across portions of northeast Iowa. These storms formed along a nearly stationary front that was in place across the region. The storms produced wind damage in Osage (Mitchell County), New Hampton (Chickasaw County) and Decorah (Winneshiek County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large tree branches were blown down in Osage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced large hail and damaging winds at several locations in northwest Iowa during the late afternoon of April 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds snapped two utility poles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak boundary extended from eastern Nebraska into southwest Iowa on the afternoon of June 27th. Thunderstorms developed along this boundary and slowly moved to the south. Although vertical wind shear was weak, strong instability was present and allowed for a few of the thunderstorms to become severe. The thunderstorms continued to develop southwest along the boundary into southeast Nebraska by early evening before the line moved into northeast Kansas and northwest Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Along with the quarter size hail that fell at the residence, the public estimated that winds were around 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed and strengthened over portions of central Nebraska and eastern Kansas through the day.  The warm front moved up into southern Minnesota while the weakening cold front existed over eastern Nebraska, leaving much of Iowa within the unstable warm sector.  Storms developed through the day along and near the warm sector, within the warm sector, and along the cold front.  Storms during the morning and early afternoon remained sub-severe, with storms mid-afternoon onward primarily producing damaging winds and heavy rainfall.  A brief tornado was reported in Bremer county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported several large limbs down and trees split in half.  The diameter of some trees were around 24 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A sagging cold front in central and southern Minnesota initiated a number of storms on the afternoon of the 10th.  While the boundary did not continue its march southward into Iowa, the storms continued to progress southeastward, eventually entering Iowa.  As a result, the storms produced heavy rainfall along with a handful of hail and wind reports.  On the afternoon of the 11th, the cold front sagged down into northern Iowa and initiated a couple of storms that produced an instance of damaging winds. In addition to the storms, a death of an infant occurred from the heat by being left in an unattended vehicle on the afternoon of the 11th in Polk County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Manager reported wind gusts up to 70 mph along with large hail, size unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front traversed the state through the day, sparking off showers and storms along the way.  A few storms in southwest and central Iowa were on the strong side, with a couple of storms producing hail just under one inch and a pair of severe wind reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS employee measured 62 mph wind gust on personal weather station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with high winds and extremely heavy rain moved across northeast Iowa during the evening of September 21st into the early morning of the 22nd. Winds of 60 to 70 mph occurred near Rockford (Floyd County) and Nora Springs (Floyd County). A small tornado touched down west of Nashua (Floyd County) and produced some crop damage. Flash flooding caused water to cover most roads over the southern two-thirds of Floyd County along with the southern sections of Chickasaw County and mudslides occurred across Allamakee County. The Governor of Iowa signed a disaster proclamation because of all the flooding in September for Allamakee, Chickasaw, Clayton, Fayette, Floyd, Howard, Mitchell and Winneshiek Counties and a federal disaster declaration was made for the same counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 66 mph was measured south of Nora Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced damaging winds at several locations, as well as large hail and flash flooding at one place each, in northwest Iowa on the evening of June 17th into the post midnight hours of June 18th.

Thunderstorms with high winds and extremely heavy rain moved across northeast Iowa during the evening of September 21st into the early morning of the 22nd. Winds of 60 to 70 mph occurred near Rockford (Floyd County) and Nora Springs (Floyd County). A small tornado touched down west of Nashua (Floyd County) and produced some crop damage. Flash flooding caused water to cover most roads over the southern two-thirds of Floyd County along with the southern sections of Chickasaw County and mudslides occurred across Allamakee County. The Governor of Iowa signed a disaster proclamation because of all the flooding in September for Allamakee, Chickasaw, Clayton, Fayette, Floyd, Howard, Mitchell and Winneshiek Counties and a federal disaster declaration was made for the same counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down in Nora Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The cold front that initiated severe weather across central and southern Iowa the previous day stalled mid-way across the state, keeping conditions fairly primed for storms. A weak area of low pressure to the west in the Colorado/Wyoming/Nebraska area developed and initiated in the early evening hours, eventually conglomerating into a potent MCS. By the time it reached Iowa, the strongest areas of the MCS resided across the northern third of the state, producing numerous damage reports and some severe hail. The line weakened through the overnight hours, eventually becoming sub-severe shortly after crossing Interstate 35.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kossuth County Emergency Manager reported extensive tree damage across the entire county due to straight line winds.  Specifically Burt, Algona, Swea City, Luverne, and Bancroft were mentioned in the report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moving southward during the day on the 2nd brought strong, gusty northwest winds to eastern Iowa. Sustained wind speeds of 30 to 40 mph with gusts as high as 60 mph caused damage to trees, power lines, and personal property.  The winds were the strongest from 7AM to 7PM CST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at the Cedar Rapids Airport reported a gusts between 40 and 50 knots from 726 AM CST through 1 PM CST. In addition to the measured gusts, Law Enforcement reported that a 3 foot diameter tree fell on the front of an SUV injuring 2 people.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter on the east side of Andrew reported a sign was snapped off a 4x4 post. Other street signs in the town were bent at a 15 to 20 degrees from the vertical.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of nocturnal storms blossomed across western and southwest Iowa, remaining relatively stationary for a couple hours before slowly moving eastward through the morning hours. Some redevelopment occurred to the north, before sliding southward and weakening into the afternoon.  In all, heavy rain was the primary result with locations in excess of 4 inches and even a 7 inch report in far southern Iowa. A pair of thunderstorm wind damage reports were associated with more organized linear storm development in west central/northwest Iowa during the morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported several trees up to one foot in diameter down near Schaller. Time and location estimated from radar and reported via social media.

Overall, most damage consisted of tree branches, with a few small trees snapped or uprooted as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms develop late in the evening of July 17 in response to the nocturnal strengthening of the low-level jet over a stalled frontal boundary over southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa. Although the majority of these thunderstorms were strong, only isolated reports of severe weather were observed mainly in the form of strong winds. In addition to the severe weather, the orientation of the thunderstorms from northwest to southeast resulted in repeated or training of the storms and heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense cyclone moved through the central and northern Plains on Christmas. This brought unusual weather to eastern Nebraska and west central Iowa for late December. Strong southerly winds ahead of the system brought unseasonably warm and moist air northward into the region allowing for non-severe thunderstorms to cross the area early in the day on Christmas. By afternoon, a strong front swept across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa bringing a line of strong thunderstorms. A couple of the thunderstorms were strong enough to produce isolated severe weather with damaging winds. After the passage of the thunderstorms and as the low pressure moved into South Dakota a strong cold front swept through the area with very strong damaging non-thunderstorm winds across northeast Nebraska in west central Iowa. This brought widespread tree and power line damage, as well as some minor structural damage to the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer indicated that strong winds produced tree damage across mainly the northern half of the county. Winds were estimated near 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure brought strong thunderstorms over Iowa and Illinois the evening of October 6th, 2016. Just ahead of the low's path, a warm front was positioned over Iowa and Illinois near Interstate 80. This allowed for winds supporting tornadoes to develop with the strongest storms. A cluster of strong to severe storms moved from near Ottumwa Iowa, northeast through Scott county, with several areas of damaging winds and tornadoes along the path from Louisa and Muscatine counties, through Scott county, and into portions of Rock Island county, Illinois before ending.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm survey team found uprooted hardwood trees due to straight line winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant early spring winter storm affected a good portion of eastern Nebraska and western Iowa during the afternoon on the 23rd into the early morning hours of the 24th. As the strong storm moved into the Plains during the morning hours on the 23rd, Gulf moisture rushed northward ahead of the system and allowed for thunderstorms and isolated severe weather to occur during the afternoon on the 23rd. On the back side of the system though, colder air fed in from the northern Plains and allowed for the rain to mix with and change over to snow over northeast Nebraska and extreme west central Iowa during the late afternoon and especially during the evening. Heavy snow was then observed in a band from northwest Iowa into northeast Nebraska with some thundersnow also observed. Extremely heavy snowfall rates of 2 to 4 inches an hour occurred within this band leading to many snowfall totals over 1 foot in a relatively short amount of time. The storm system finally started to shift out of the area during the early morning hours on the 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A barn was reportedly destroyed and blow into a house as this address.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system situated to the north of the state proceeded east-southeast out of the Dakotas and into Minnesota through the day.  The accompanying cold front, while not excessively cold, did come with a 850mb jet of around 70kts.  Resulting sustained winds approached 40 mph in many locations across northern Iowa, including a number of mesonet and ASOS sites reporting wind gusts at or in excess of 58 mph in the mid to late morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ankeny RWIS measured 60 mph wind gust and the DSM ASOS also measured a 60 mph wind.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of the 25th, a cold front progressed into portions of central and eastern Nebraska and eventually stalled out in eastern Nebraska.  During the evening hours storms that initiated earlier in the day in western Nebraska continued to make their way across Nebraska and into Iowa and produced a number of high wind, damaging wind, and hail reports.  Prior to the main line, a severe storm fired up in northern Iowa in Emmet and produced near severe hail and severe winds. After the main line pushed through in the overnight hours, a stalled front in northern Missouri aided in ushering in another round of heavy rainfall across southern Iowa, where reports upwards of 4 plus inches were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estherville Municipal airport ASOS station reported peak wind of 67 kts.

After the main line pushed through in the overnight hours, a stalled front in northern Missouri aided in ushering in another round of heavy rainfall across southern Iowa, where reports upwards of 4 plus inches were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported several small tree limbs down with some 5 to 6 inch diameter branches as well.  Pea sized hail also observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Four tornadoes occurred in southwest Iowa as an upper low moved through the upper Midwest. Some hail was reported across portions of southern Iowa along with isolated wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A semi was reported flipped and a shed destroyed along Hwy 34.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of the 25th, a cold front progressed into portions of central and eastern Nebraska and eventually stalled out in eastern Nebraska.  During the evening hours storms that initiated earlier in the day in western Nebraska continued to make their way across Nebraska and into Iowa and produced a number of high wind, damaging wind, and hail reports.  Prior to the main line, a severe storm fired up in northern Iowa in Emmet and produced near severe hail and severe winds. After the main line pushed through in the overnight hours, a stalled front in northern Missouri aided in ushering in another round of heavy rainfall across southern Iowa, where reports upwards of 4 plus inches were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported winds of at least 60 mph that blew down half the trees in their front yard.  Time radar estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low meandered across South Dakota and into central Minnesota through the day of the 11th.  The leading warm front lifted northward through Iowa and into southern Minnesota, firmly placing the state within the warm sector.  That evening, storms initiated along/near the cold front in eastern Nebraska, eventually forming a QLCS that progressed into and through the state.  While the system was at its peak primarily west of Interstate 35, severe weather was reported as far east as Plainfield, in Bremer County.  The majority of reports were related to thunderstorm wind damage, though small hail, including a severe hail report, was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported large tree limbs downed near intersection of 190th street and Baxter Ave west of Barnum.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported 4 to 5 inch diameter tree down via social media.  Delayed report and time radar estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of storms worked their way across Nebraska overnight on the 18th into the 19th, eventually making their way into Iowa. As those elevated storms approached the central third of Iowa, the linear storms began to backbuild and with increasing convection behind them in association with ongoing warm air advection.  Through the morning hours ample lightning was associated with the storms along with heavy rainfall around 3 inches in the Fort Dodge area and 4 to 6 inches in the Des Moines area. A number of flash flooding events were reported as a result of the heavy rainfall.  During the afternoon, the storms resumed their forward propagation and increased in strength, especially across southern and southeast Iowa.  Damaging winds and a couple of tornado reports were recorded across south central Iowa along with rainfall in the 1 to 3 inch range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law Enforcement reported downed trees up to 24 inches between Lacona and Milo.  Time and location estimated from radar.  Delayed report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Through the day of the 5th, a warm front lifted through the region increasing temperatures and dew points, priming conditions for an impending cold front.  That evening, as the low pressure center lifted into southern Minnesota, the trailing cold front entered the state. It initiated storms across northeast Iowa that remained sub-severe while in the Des Moines WFO service area, as well as a series of storms in eastern Nebraska that eventually made their way across central Iowa.  Those storms primarily produced damaging winds and severe wind gusts along with periods of heavy rainfall.  After the initial cluster of storms moved through and weakened overnight, new development along the sagging cold front produced a morning round of damaging winds and heavy rainfall across southern and southeast Iowa.  Lastly, during the afternoon, conditions were primed for possible funnel cloud/landspout development, and was seen in Tama County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sheriff reported half of the city of Carroll without power and numerous crews out clearing up to a foot diameter trees from roads in town along with power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Through much of the day of the 16th, a warm front slowly progressed into the state from the west, orienting itself north-south about a third to half of the way into the state.  As a result, storms were able to initiate and provide periods of heavy rainfall, numerous damaging wind reports, and a handful of severe hail reports.  Heavy rainfall amounts approached 2 to 3 inches across northern Iowa. The majority of severe wind reports occurred between 2 am and 4:30 am, heavy rainfall reports after around 7 am, and hail reports during redevelopment during the mid-late morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law Enforcement reported an uprooted tree.  Time radar estimated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crawford County dispatch reported power lines down.  Time radar estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The cold front that initiated severe weather across central and southern Iowa the previous day stalled mid-way across the state, keeping conditions fairly primed for storms. A weak area of low pressure to the west in the Colorado/Wyoming/Nebraska area developed and initiated in the early evening hours, eventually conglomerating into a potent MCS. By the time it reached Iowa, the strongest areas of the MCS resided across the northern third of the state, producing numerous damage reports and some severe hail. The line weakened through the overnight hours, eventually becoming sub-severe shortly after crossing Interstate 35.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Media reported 12 inch diameter tree limbs down.  Time radar estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the early morning hours of May 26th across northeast Iowa. These storms formed ahead of a cold front and produced strong winds across portions of Mitchell, Fayette and Clayton Counties. In Oelwein (Fayette County), a 62 mph wind gust was measured by the automated weather observing equipment at the airport. Several barns were damaged by the high winds near Arlington (Fayette County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 62 mph wind gust was measured by the automated weather observing equipment at the Oelwein airport.  A 30 inch diameter tree was blown down in Oelwein.  Power outages also occurred from downed lines and damage to transformers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An ideal setup was seen across northern and central Iowa for repeated rounds of heavy rainfall and severe weather.  A weak warm frontal boundary stalled out across the area as it transitioned to a stationary boundary and allowed multiple rounds of storms to initiate on the 21st and well into the 22nd.  Primarily heavy rainfall and flooding was seen, however a couple of hail and a handful of damaging wind reports were received as well.  The hardest hit areas were from roughly Forest City on southeast towards Mason city and Waverly. Rainfall amounts of 4 to 6 plus inches were seen, with greatest amounts just outside of the Des Moines county warning area in and around Charles City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported damage to several large trees.  Time estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong to severe thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front that pushed eastward across Iowa during the late afternoon and early evening hours. These storms brought damaging winds, torrential rain, and frequent lightning to the area.  Several downed trees, power outages, and 40 to 60 mph winds were reported.  Widespread rainfall amounts between 0.75 to 1.5 inches of rain fell in a short amount of time.  This produced flash flooding in the city of Clinton in Clinton County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported an estimated wind gust around 60 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few thunderstorms initiated ahead of a cold front through portions of central Iowa, within a warm and moist environment, ripe with dew points into mid and upper 70s.  A couple of the morning thunderstorms were able to produce damaging winds, while thunderstorms associated with the cold front later that evening produced a few more damaging wind reports.  Overall, most damage consisted of tree branches, with a few small trees snapped or uprooted as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Media reported a large tree down on some power lines.  Delayed report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During a hot and muggy afternoon, where temperatures topped out around 90 with dew points close to 80, thunderstorms were able to initiate through central Iowa.  With supportive low level environments for funnel clouds and winds, a handful of related reports were received, along with periods of heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Des Moines International Airport ASOS station recorded a 61 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced damaging winds at a few locations in Woodbury County of northwest Iowa on the late afternoon of August 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused tree damage, including one tree blown down onto a highway.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew over a grain bin.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew over a service station canopy and caused tree damage, including 7 inch branches blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with locally heavy rain moved across portions of northeast Iowa throughout the day on July 23rd. The heavy rain caused the Upper Iowa River to send water over County Road W14 near Kendallville (Winneshiek County). The Paradise Valley Campground (Clayton County) sustained extensive damage and had to be evacuated from flooding along a branch of the Sny Magill Creek. Buildings and roads were damaged with picnic tables destroyed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and branches were blown down in Lansing. Boat docks were damaged when the wind pushed some boats several feet upstream and onto the shore.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary frontal boundary spawned a line of thunderstorms that produced numerous reports of damaging winds and isolated tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local law enforcement reported significant damage to a building with nearby trees twisted and some farm equipment knocked over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system situated to the north of the state proceeded east-southeast out of the Dakotas and into Minnesota through the day.  The accompanying cold front, while not excessively cold, did come with a 850mb jet of around 70kts.  Resulting sustained winds approached 40 mph in many locations across northern Iowa, including a number of mesonet and ASOS sites reporting wind gusts at or in excess of 58 mph in the mid to late morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 40 mph or more based on area mesonet, ASOS, and AWOS sites.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system resided in southeastern Nebraska much of the day, slowly moving eastward towards the Iowa border.  The warm front progressed into central Iowa before stalling out in the afternoon and evening hours.  The cold front trailed back in southeastern Nebraska and down through eastern Kansas.  Weak convection began in western and northwest Iowa late in the afternoon with little to no hail or high winds, before more widespread convection fired as the low pressure center approached the Iowa-Nebraska border in the evening hours.  With the warm front stalled out and generally draped along/near Interstate 80, storms that were able to root themselves along there were the strongest and produced primarily severe or borderline severe hail and high winds.  Many hail reports were of the pea sized variety, with a handful of severe/one inch hail reports from storms to the west of Des Moines and across the northern portions of the Des Moines metro as storms moved across.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported wind gusts of up to 61 mph via their home weather station.  Also reported damage to roof flashing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As temperatures warmed into the upper 80s to near 90, isolated to scattered thunderstorms developed over far eastern Nebraska and western Iowa. Although shear was very marginal, sufficient instability was present for a few stronger storms and very isolated reports of severe weather. The thunderstorms quickly weakened during the late evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Some 2 inch in diameter limbs and power lines were reported down in Clarinda.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Denison Airport AWOS reported peak wind gust of 76 kts.  Law Enforcement also reported numerous tree limbs down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of the 25th, a cold front progressed into portions of central and eastern Nebraska and eventually stalled out in eastern Nebraska.  During the evening hours storms that initiated earlier in the day in western Nebraska continued to make their way across Nebraska and into Iowa and produced a number of high wind, damaging wind, and hail reports.  Prior to the main line, a severe storm fired up in northern Iowa in Emmet and produced near severe hail and severe winds. After the main line pushed through in the overnight hours, a stalled front in northern Missouri aided in ushering in another round of heavy rainfall across southern Iowa, where reports upwards of 4 plus inches were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law Enforcement and Emergency Management reported several 3 to 5 inches tree limbs down throughout the town of Estherville.  Some tree limbs were blocking roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced large hail at several locations in northwest Iowa during the late afternoon and early evening of May 23rd. The storms also produce damaging winds at one location, flash flooding at another, and an isolated tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused tree damage, including up to 6 inch diameter branches broken off. The winds also blew weather equipment off a garage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system progressing southeast passed just northeast of the state.  With a tight surface pressure gradient and the associated cold front pushing through in the morning hours of April 2nd, strong winds were realized through much of the northern portions of central Iowa.  A wind advisory was in effect for much of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported large tree limbs/branches down with limbs greater than 2 inches.  Limbs snapped from an oak tree and landed on top of a home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of showers with no thunder observed moved across the southern half of the state.  An AWOS station in Mahaska county did record a 60 mph wind gust, as one of the showers moved through the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Oskaloosa Municipal Airport AWOS station recorded a 60 mph wind gust as a shower moved through the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Through much of the day of the 16th, a warm front slowly progressed into the state from the west, orienting itself north-south about a third to half of the way into the state.  As a result, storms were able to initiate and provide periods of heavy rainfall, numerous damaging wind reports, and a handful of severe hail reports.  Heavy rainfall amounts approached 2 to 3 inches across northern Iowa. The majority of severe wind reports occurred between 2 am and 4:30 am, heavy rainfall reports after around 7 am, and hail reports during redevelopment during the mid-late morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law Enforcement reported several large trees and power lines down in Steamboat Rock.  At least 2 power poles snapped in half just west of Steamboat Rock on D35.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low meandered across South Dakota and into central Minnesota through the day of the 11th.  The leading warm front lifted northward through Iowa and into southern Minnesota, firmly placing the state within the warm sector.  That evening, storms initiated along/near the cold front in eastern Nebraska, eventually forming a QLCS that progressed into and through the state.  While the system was at its peak primarily west of Interstate 35, severe weather was reported as far east as Plainfield, in Bremer County.  The majority of reports were related to thunderstorm wind damage, though small hail, including a severe hail report, was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported numerous trees down and uprooted.  A large grain bin was pushed several feet onto the railroad tracks.  Large trash dumpsters were also tipped over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A front trailing low pressure near Hudson Bay generally stalled out across the state, initially across northern Iowa before progressing southward as a cold front later in the day.  Storms that initiated near the front in northwest Iowa in the early morning hours slowly strengthened and progressed east southeast through the morning and early afternoon hours, eventually producing golf ball sized hail and a hand full of damaging wind reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported oak tree snapped off at its base and other tree damage and branches down in the immediate area.  Delayed report and time radar estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of storms worked their way across Nebraska overnight on the 18th into the 19th, eventually making their way into Iowa. As those elevated storms approached the central third of Iowa, the linear storms began to backbuild and with increasing convection behind them in association with ongoing warm air advection.  Through the morning hours ample lightning was associated with the storms along with heavy rainfall around 3 inches in the Fort Dodge area and 4 to 6 inches in the Des Moines area. A number of flash flooding events were reported as a result of the heavy rainfall.  During the afternoon, the storms resumed their forward propagation and increased in strength, especially across southern and southeast Iowa.  Damaging winds and a couple of tornado reports were recorded across south central Iowa along with rainfall in the 1 to 3 inch range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported damaged brick and concrete silos, barn damage, roof damage, and tree damage.  Time radar estimated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law Enforcement reported a large tree downed on top of a vehicle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The cold front that initiated severe weather across central and southern Iowa the previous day stalled mid-way across the state, keeping conditions fairly primed for storms. A weak area of low pressure to the west in the Colorado/Wyoming/Nebraska area developed and initiated in the early evening hours, eventually conglomerating into a potent MCS. By the time it reached Iowa, the strongest areas of the MCS resided across the northern third of the state, producing numerous damage reports and some severe hail. The line weakened through the overnight hours, eventually becoming sub-severe shortly after crossing Interstate 35.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Manager reported a camper overturned due to high winds.  Time radar estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Through the day of the 5th, a warm front lifted through the region increasing temperatures and dew points, priming conditions for an impending cold front.  That evening, as the low pressure center lifted into southern Minnesota, the trailing cold front entered the state. It initiated storms across northeast Iowa that remained sub-severe while in the Des Moines WFO service area, as well as a series of storms in eastern Nebraska that eventually made their way across central Iowa.  Those storms primarily produced damaging winds and severe wind gusts along with periods of heavy rainfall.  After the initial cluster of storms moved through and weakened overnight, new development along the sagging cold front produced a morning round of damaging winds and heavy rainfall across southern and southeast Iowa.  Lastly, during the afternoon, conditions were primed for possible funnel cloud/landspout development, and was seen in Tama County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law Enforcement reported large tree branches down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of storms that developed in Nebraska near a parent low pressure system the night before progressed eastward through the overnight and morning hours, eventually making its way across northern Iowa. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported several tree branches down in the vicinity, up to 4 inches in diameter.  Delayed report and time radar estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During a hot and muggy afternoon, where temperatures topped out around 90 with dew points close to 80, thunderstorms were able to initiate through central Iowa.  With supportive low level environments for funnel clouds and winds, a handful of related reports were received, along with periods of heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported several 3 to 5 inch diameter tree branches down throughout Rockwell City.  Delayed report and time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced damaging winds at Hull in Sioux County of northwest Iowa before daybreak on the morning of August 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused tree damage, including several trees blown down. Some of the fallen trees blocked a road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong to severe thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front that pushed eastward across Iowa during the late afternoon and early evening hours. These storms brought damaging winds, torrential rain, and frequent lightning to the area.  Several downed trees, power outages, and 40 to 60 mph winds were reported.  Widespread rainfall amounts between 0.75 to 1.5 inches of rain fell in a short amount of time.  This produced flash flooding in the city of Clinton in Clinton County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A state official reported numerous tree limbs down where Highway 52 comes into Dubuque. Some of the limbs were down homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A boundary oriented southwest to northeast slowly made its way into and across Iowa from the northwest through the 19th and early into the 20th.  The boundary generally stalled over central Iowa mid morning and into the evening, allowing storms to initiate and train over the same areas much of the day before becoming more progressive later.  Resulting issues were primarily heavy rainfall in excess of 2 inches, along with a couple of severe wind reports and a flash flood report.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported at least two large trees down in Nevada.  Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced large hail, damaging winds, and heavy rain in Woodbury and Ida Counties of northwest Iowa during the late morning and early afternoon of August 23rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused tree damage, blew down power lines, and blew out windows at a bank.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced large hail, damaging winds, and heavy rain in Woodbury and Ida Counties of northwest Iowa during the late morning and early afternoon of August 23rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds damaged trees and crops.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced large hail, damaging winds, and heavy rain in Woodbury and Ida Counties of northwest Iowa during the late morning and early afternoon of August 23rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused tree damage, including a few trees blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small complex of thunderstorms traveled northeast through Kansas and Nebraska overnight the 22nd into the 23rd, finally arriving early in the afternoon.  With instability building with daytime heating, the storms were able to strengthen and grow in spatial coverage as they traversed northwest/northern Iowa.  While no severe reports were made with the initial storms, heavy rainfall was reported.  Later in the day, as a cold front approached, more storms were able to initiate and provide additional heavy rainfall as well as a couple of damaging wind reports.  Most reports resided across northern Iowa, while the damaging winds occurred during the evening more towards southern Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported several tree limbs upwards of 8 inches in diameter downed. Additionally, a hollowed out 15 to 20 inch diameter tree downed. Over 1 inch of rainfall reported in half an hour as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few thunderstorms initiated ahead of a cold front through portions of central Iowa, within a warm and moist environment, ripe with dew points into mid and upper 70s.  A couple of the morning thunderstorms were able to produce damaging winds, while thunderstorms associated with the cold front later that evening produced a few more damaging wind reports.  Overall, most damage consisted of tree branches, with a few small trees snapped or uprooted as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported estimated 65 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a cold front moved across the Upper Midwest, thunderstorms developed across the region. The storms that moved across portions of Northeast Iowa produced damaging winds with trees blown down in Decorah (Winneshiek County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees were blow down near Kendallville. Some farm buildings and a grain bin were also damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a cold front moved across the Upper Midwest, thunderstorms developed across the region. The storms that moved across portions of Northeast Iowa produced damaging winds with trees blown down in Decorah (Winneshiek County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The automated weather observing equipment at the Decorah airport measured a wind gust of 63 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved moved out of northeast Kansas and progressed northeast up through Iowa during the morning and afternoon hours of the 18th.  On the backside, a strong pressure gradient developed with the aide of high pressure slowly moving into the Plains off the Rockies.  As a result, a number of areas saw wind gusts in excess of 40 mph and sustained winds in excess of 25 mph.  Northern Iowa saw the strongest winds, including an automated report of 59 mph.  Additionally, tree damage was reported in central Iowa, just southwest of the Des Moines metro.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estherville ASOS recorded a 59 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the evening of July 11th and during the early morning hours of July 12th across northeast Iowa. These storms formed ahead of a cold front that was approaching the region. Some of these storms briefly produced damaging wind gusts that destroyed a grain bin, blew the roof off a machine shed and uprooted trees southwest of Mitchell (Mitchell County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A grain bin was destroyed, the roof was blown off a machine shed and trees were uprooted on a farm southwest of Mitchell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moving just north of Iowa, brought a very strong cold front through Iowa and Illinois during the mid morning through afternoon of February 19th.  Behind this cold front, sustained west winds of 35 to 40 mph gusted frequently to 60 to 63 mph over all of eastern Iowa and northern Illinois found generally along and north of Interstate 80.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: AWOS observations from eastern Iowa airports along and north of Interstate 80 began reporting winds gusts of 50 to 60 mph around 11 am and continued through around 3 pm. The peak winds for all areas occurred around 1 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed and strengthened over portions of central Nebraska and eastern Kansas through the day.  The warm front moved up into southern Minnesota while the weakening cold front existed over eastern Nebraska, leaving much of Iowa within the unstable warm sector.  Storms developed through the day along and near the warm sector, within the warm sector, and along the cold front.  Storms during the morning and early afternoon remained sub-severe, with storms mid-afternoon onward primarily producing damaging winds and heavy rainfall.  A brief tornado was reported in Bremer county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported via social media four inch tree limbs broken in town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wave riding the northwest upper level flow through the Dakotas and Northern Minnesota initiated a series of storms that eventually topped the ridge and started southeast.  The storms eventually initiated along an outflow boundary, taking them due south into northern Iowa, where they produced periods of heavy rain and a few damaging and high wind reports near Estherville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law Enforcement in Emmet County reported a large pine tree down in Wallingford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The state was stuck under northwest flow aloft, resulting in shortwaves aloft initiating storms.  Additionally, at the surface a warm frontal boundary resided across the southwest third to half of the state during the day of the 21st and through the overnight.  Some storms initially developed during the afternoon and evening hours in the immediate vicinity of the front in southern and central Iowa and produced periods of heavy rainfall.  Storms primarily resulted in heavy rainfall and a couple of severe wind reports.  The primary action initiated late in the evening across the northwest third of the state, in the vicinity of the warm front as it moved northward.  Given northwest steering flow and a nearly station surface front, storms trained through the evening and resulted in rainfall totals as high as 3.71 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law Enforcement reported a large tree branch downed onto a law enforcement vehicle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak area of low pressure developed over northeast Nebraska during the afternoon of June 29th. To the east of the low, a weak frontal boundary extended into western Iowa while a surface trough extended south from the low through eastern Nebraska. Isolated thunderstorms initially developed along the boundary in western Iowa and moved southwest producing hail, wind and an isolated tornado. Other thunderstorms developed near the Omaha area along the surface trough and moved south producing hail and wind. Thunderstorms weakened by late evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rear flank downdraft winds produced tree damage along a wind break along Highway 30.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The day was a two part event with the first coming in the morning hours across east central Iowa.  There, a shor wave moved through the upper levels, enabling a few storms to initiate and produce heavy rainfall.  Later in the day, an advancing cold front from Minnesota moved into the state, initiating storms across northern Iowa.  While most storms were sub-severe, a few reports of damaging winds were reported north of Waterloo, along with sub-severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported uprooted/downed trees near Denver.  Report via social media, relayed by KWWL.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The day was a two part event with the first coming in the morning hours across east central Iowa. Later in the day, an advancing cold front from Minnesota moved into the state, initiating storms across northern Iowa. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Manager reported several instances of tree limbs down and crop damage near Denver at 175th and Highway 63, 180th and Joplin, and 1N and 2S in Bremer County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak boundary extended from eastern Nebraska into southwest Iowa on the afternoon of June 27th. Thunderstorms developed along this boundary and slowly moved to the south. Although vertical wind shear was weak, strong instability was present and allowed for a few of the thunderstorms to become severe. The thunderstorms continued to develop southwest along the boundary into southeast Nebraska by early evening before the line moved into northeast Kansas and northwest Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trailer was blow over onto its side from the strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of thunderstorms and showers moved across the region bringing a couple of damaging wind reports and localized, short term and one medium term flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The county Emergency Manager reported several trees and large branches were down.  The time of the event was estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively weak cold front made its way into Iowa from the northwest during the daytime hours of the 19th, eventually working it's way through central Iowa by the afternoon.  A few storms initiated along and just ahead of the front during the mid-afternoon hours, though many simply could not maintain themselves within the environment.  One storm was able to sustain and strengthen, including consistent rotation and taking a 'right turn' with regards to its motion.  Resulting reports out of the storm included a funnel cloud and a handful of damaging winds reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported two instances of a large tree downed onto a house in Chariton.  Time and location estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms initiated out in the Nebraska Panhandle area the night prior, translating eastward through the night across northern Nebraska and southern South Dakota.  As the storms approached and entered northern Iowa and southern Minnesota they strengthened, expanded in aerial coverage, and turned southward along the instability gradient.  The primary results of the storms were gusty winds and heavy rainfall.  The bulk of the rainfall occurred during the evening of the 15th, and tapered off in the early hours of the 16th.  A couple of high wind gust reports were received along with numerous heavy rainfall amounts between 2 and 3 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported 58-66 mph wind gusts measured with a hand held anemometer.  Also reported several 2 inch diameter tree limbs down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms initiated out in the Nebraska Panhandle area the night prior, translating eastward through the night across northern Nebraska and southern South Dakota.  As the storms approached and entered northern Iowa and southern Minnesota they strengthened, expanded in aerial coverage, and turned southward along the instability gradient.  The primary results of the storms were gusty winds and heavy rainfall.  The bulk of the rainfall occurred during the evening of the 15th, and tapered off in the early hours of the 16th.  A couple of high wind gust reports were received along with numerous heavy rainfall amounts between 2 and 3 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Waterloo airport ASOS recorded 61 mph wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively weak cold front made its way into Iowa from the northwest during the daytime hours of the 19th, eventually working it's way through central Iowa by the afternoon.  A few storms initiated along and just ahead of the front during the mid-afternoon hours, though many simply could not maintain themselves within the environment.  One storm was able to sustain and strengthen, including consistent rotation and taking a 'right turn' with regards to its motion.  Resulting reports out of the storm included a funnel cloud and a handful of damaging winds reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wayne county emergency manager reported multiple pole barns blown over.  Debris from pole barns blew into and downed nearby power lines.  Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very hot and humid airmass resided over eastern Nebraska and western Iowa on the evening of July 5th. This led to extreme instability over the area. A cluster of thunderstorms developed over central Nebraska and moved east northeast into this atmosphere. These thunderstorms were response for wind damage, some significant, as they moved across eastern Nebraska and into west central Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines were reported downed in town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cool front spawned widespread thunderstorms resulting in numerous reports of winds between 40 to 70 MPH along and north of I-80 in Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS measured maximum wind gust at Cedar Rapids Regional Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cool front spawned widespread thunderstorms resulting in numerous reports of winds between 40 to 70 MPH along and north of I-80 in Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Received from a local Iowa Mesonet RWIS site of a measured maximum wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cool front spawned widespread thunderstorms resulting in numerous reports of winds between 40 to 70 MPH along and north of I-80 in Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Iowa City ASOS site just southwest of Iowa City measured maximum wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few small thunderstorms developed in the mid afternoon hours in a moist air-mass.  The strongest storm produced some damaging wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This was a delayed report. A large tree was downed at a farm near Elm Avenue and 135th street about 2 miles west southwest of Wellman. The tree fell onto a minivan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure brought strong thunderstorms over Iowa and Illinois the evening of October 6th, 2016. Just ahead of the low's path, a warm front was positioned over Iowa and Illinois near Interstate 80. This allowed for winds supporting tornadoes to develop with the strongest storms. A cluster of strong to severe storms moved from near Ottumwa Iowa, northeast through Scott county, with several areas of damaging winds and tornadoes along the path from Louisa and Muscatine counties, through Scott county, and into portions of Rock Island county, Illinois before ending.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm survey team indicated hardwood tree branches uprooted due to straight line winds. Additional large tree branch damage was seen in the area just to the east.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm survey team found a small area of scattered straight line wind damage to two farmsteads at this location. Large hardwood tree branch damage as well as minor damage to a farm outbuilding roof was surveyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cool front spawned widespread thunderstorms resulting in numerous reports of winds between 40 to 70 MPH along and north of I-80 in Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many tree limbs over 3 inches in diameter were blown down near 42nd and  Division Street. An 8-inch  diameter tree was also snapped off near its base.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moving just north of Iowa, brought a very strong cold front through Iowa and Illinois during the mid morning through afternoon of February 19th.  Behind this cold front, sustained west winds of 35 to 40 mph gusted frequently to 60 to 63 mph over all of eastern Iowa and northern Illinois found generally along and north of Interstate 80.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: ASOS and AWOS observations from eastern Iowa airports along and north of Interstate 80 began reporting winds gusts of 50 to 60 mph around 11 am and continued through around 3 pm. The peak winds for all areas occurred around 1 pm. In addition, reports of damage occurring with these strong winds include a semi trailer blown over on Highway 61 near Park View, apartment rain gutter and deck railing damage in Davenport,  and a light pole blown over onto a car in Davenport.  Minor damage to a Ferris Wheel in Davenport was reported as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moving just north of Iowa, brought a very strong cold front through Iowa and Illinois during the mid morning through afternoon of February 19th. EVENT_NARRATIVE: ASOS observations from eastern Iowa airports along and north of Interstate 80 began reporting winds gusts of 50 to 63 mph around 11 am and continued through around 3 pm. The peak winds for all areas occurred around 1 pm. A tree was partially uprooted at the University of Iowa Campus.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds, not associated with thunderstorms, caused tree damage near the town of Spirit Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds not associated with thunderstorms caused tree damage in and near Spirit Lake, including large branches blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front traversed the state through the day, sparking off showers and storms along the way.  A few storms in southwest and central Iowa were on the strong side, with a couple of storms producing hail just under one inch and a pair of severe wind reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law Enforcement reported machine shed destroyed just south of New Providence.  Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A sagging cold front in central and southern Minnesota initiated a number of storms on the afternoon of the 10th.  While the boundary did not continue its march southward into Iowa, the storms continued to progress southeastward, eventually entering Iowa.  As a result, the storms produced heavy rainfall along with a handful of hail and wind reports.  On the afternoon of the 11th, the cold front sagged down into northern Iowa and initiated a couple of storms that produced an instance of damaging winds. In addition to the storms, a death of an infant occurred from the heat by being left in an unattended vehicle on the afternoon of the 11th in Polk County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported large oak tree topped with section greater than one foot in diameter down.  Three other oak trees with large limbs down as well.  Estimated winds in excess of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed and strengthened over portions of central Nebraska and eastern Kansas through the day.  The warm front moved up into southern Minnesota while the weakening cold front existed over eastern Nebraska, leaving much of Iowa within the unstable warm sector.  Storms developed through the day along and near the warm sector, within the warm sector, and along the cold front.  Storms during the morning and early afternoon remained sub-severe, with storms mid-afternoon onward primarily producing damaging winds and heavy rainfall.  A brief tornado was reported in Bremer county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estherville airport ASOS recorded 73 mph wind gust.  It also recorded a 60 mph wind gust 1 minute after as well.

Storms during the morning and early afternoon remained sub-severe, with storms mid-afternoon onward primarily producing damaging winds and heavy rainfall.  A brief tornado was reported in Bremer county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported a moderately sized cattle shed was destroyed along with tree branches down around the homestead.  A grain bin was also blown a half-mile into a nearby field.  Another shed was badly damaged at a nearby homestead.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This event was a two part event for many.  Initially, storms developed in the Dakotas near the parent cold front, and proceeded to develop into an MCS and move into and across Iowa during the morning hours of the 11th.  Heavy rainfall and a couple damaging wind reports were reported.  During the afternoon and evening of the 11th, the parent frontal boundary slowly meandered into Iowa, initiating another round of storms. As the evening wore on it become more progressive and the leading MCS produced numerous damaging wind reports and additional heavy rainfall.  The greatest rainfall amounts reported exceeded 5 inches, and were primarily located north of Interstate 80.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported multiple trees and tree limbs down in Stuart and Menlo.  Largest tree was 12 inches in diameter.  Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of showers and thunderstorms moved across eastern Iowa during the morning hours of August 11th. These storms produced gusty winds of 50 to 60 mph that caused damaged to trees in the city of Davenport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Davenport Public Works reported trees down on Locust and Grant Streets in the city. The time of the event was estimated using radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A boundary oriented southwest to northeast slowly made its way into and across Iowa from the northwest through the 19th and early into the 20th.  The boundary generally stalled over central Iowa mid morning and into the evening, allowing storms to initiate and train over the same areas much of the day before becoming more progressive later.  Resulting issues were primarily heavy rainfall in excess of 2 inches, along with a couple of severe wind reports and a flash flood report.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported 50 to 60 mph winds with small hail stones about the size of dimes. Winds lasted roughly 3 to 4 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak frontal boundary draped itself across the state the previous day and into the 28th, oscillating roughly between northern and southern Iowa.  During the morning and early afternoon of the 28th the boundary had made its way into southern Iowa, allowing storms to initiate and train in the area. Given the thermodynamic and wind profiles, a little bit of everything was able to occur.  Ranging from flash flooding, a funnel cloud, sub-severe hail, and damaging winds. While a bit of everything occurred, it was all very low end by severe weather standards.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fire department reported 6 to 8 inch tree limb down on a powerline at SW 1st and Davis Ave.  Time radar estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and humid air mass remained in place across eastern Iowa during the afternoon and early evening of August 29, 2016. Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed during the afternoon with one thunderstorm producing winds that belw down a healthy tree near DeWitt in Clinton County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A COOP Observer that a healthy tree was blown down at their residence during a thunderstorm. Time of the event was estimated using radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong to severe thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front that pushed eastward across Iowa during the late afternoon and early evening hours. These storms brought damaging winds, torrential rain, and frequent lightning to the area.  Several downed trees, power outages, and 40 to 60 mph winds were reported.  Widespread rainfall amounts between 0.75 to 1.5 inches of rain fell in a short amount of time.  This produced flash flooding in the city of Clinton in Clinton County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fire Department reported that a steel roof was blown off of a building across the the street from the Fire Station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front slowly worked south into northeast Nebraska and west central Iowa during the afternoon of August 11th as a weak storm system crossed the northern Plains. Although wind shear was weak, strong instability developed across the area as temperatures soared into the 90s with dew points in the 70s. Thunderstorms developed along the cold front in northeast Nebraska by late afternoon and moved southeast across west central Iowa and eastern Nebraska. Other thunderstorms also developed in south central Nebraska and north central Kansas and moved into southeast Nebraska. Isolated severe weather was observed with both lines of thunderstorms, along with heavy rainfall totals.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measurement was from the Iowa Department of Transportation.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported 7 inch diameter tree snapped at the base.  Some 5 inch diameter trees partially uprooted or leaning.  Up to 90 percent of fruit crop stripped off trees.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The emergency manager reported partial trees were down.  The time was estimated based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary frontal boundary spawned a line of thunderstorms that produced numerous reports of damaging winds and isolated tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter estimated winds of 70 to 75 mph based on the movement of the trees.   No damage was visible at the time of the report.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS employee reported large branches were downed by the high wind.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A COOP Observer reported a Mulberry tree was blown over, numerous other tree limbs were down.
